coredata.dc:title: Achieving tunable time-dependent delayed emission color via dynamic competition between the matrix and carbon dots for anti-counterfeiting and multidimensional encryption
coredata.dc:creator: Chang, Yufeng
Fu, Nian
Chen, Mingjun
Zhang, You
Li, Fuxing
Liu, Zhenyang
Wang, Fenghe
Yue, Gang
Li, Xu
Guan, Li
coredata.dc:description: Delayed emission (DE) materials with tunable lifetime and wavelength can provide more modes of anti-counterfeiting and information encryption for improving security levels. It is both attractive and challenging to create carbon-based materials with dynamic DE colors through multiple luminescence centers. Herein, we have developed a composite material (BA@CDs) with tunable lifetimes and variable DE colors ranging from blue to orange by heating treatment method. Surprisingly, the delayed luminescence emission of BA@CDs can last up to 11 s, and then a color change occurs within 2 s. By utilizing various analysis methods, the origin of this long lifetime DE property is investigated, revealing the significant role played by two distinct phosphorescent emission centers. The dynamic competition and energy transfer (ET) process between the two emission centers reveal an interaction between the host matrix and N, S-CDs, indicating a significant interplay between them. This tunable phosphorescent material further demonstrates its advantages in anti-counterfeiting and information encryption.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Phosphorescence
Thermally activated delayed fluorescence
Anti-counterfeiting
Encryption

xocs:hub-sec.xocs:hub-sec-title: Novel Materials for Energy and Advanced Applications

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was partly funded by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 62175075 , No. 62075055 , No. 61974009 ), the Central Project Guide local science and technology for development of Hebei Province (226Z1103G) and the Interdisciplinary research project of Hebei University (No. DXK202012). We also appreciate the technology support from the high-performance supercomputer center of Hebei University.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Abs, PL and PLE spectra of CDs. Inset: the optical graph of CDs solution under day light (left) and 455 nm light (right); (b) FT-IR pattern of CDs; (c) TEM image of CDs. Inset: HR-TEM image of CDs; High-resolution XPS spectra of C (d), N (e)and S (f).

(a) TEM image of BA@CDs; (b) HR-TEM image of BA@CDs; (c) HAADF-STEM image of BA@CDs and the corresponding elemental mapping images for B, O, C, N and S; (d) XRD patterns of N, S-CDs, BA@CDs and B2O3; (e) FT-IR pattern of BA@CDs; High-resolution XPS spectra of (f) B and (g) C.

(a) Images of BA@CDs powders before and after turning off the excitation source (365 nm and 455 nm); (b) Time-resolved emission spectra of BA@CDs under the excitation wavelength of 365 nm; (c) PL spectra of BA@CDs under different excitation wavelength; (d) DE spectra of BA@CDs under various excitation wavelength; DE spectra of BA@CDs and P-BA at the excitation source of (e) 260 nm, (f) 360 nm and (g) 420 nm.

2D pseudo-color contour map of normalized DE spectra of BA@CDs as functions of temperature under the excitation wavelength of (a) 420, (d) 360 and (g) 260 nm; Integrated DE spectra intensity (orange line) and DE spectra peak position (blue line) of BA@CDs as a function of temperature under the excitation wavelength of (b) 420, (e) 360 and (h) 260 nm; The DE spectra of BA@CDs under the excitation wavelength of (c) 420, (f) 360 and (i) 260 nm fitted by three emission peaks, respectively. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

DE decay curve and lifetimes of BA@CDs under the excitation wavelength of (a) 260 nm, (b) 360 nm and (c) 420 nm; Schematic representations of the luminescent mechanism (d) in high energy, (e) in low energy.

(a) The time-dependent DE color photographs of Hebei University’s emblem pattern; (b) Application of anti-counterfeiting ink; (c) Photographs of BA calcined product doped with different concentrations of CDs after moving the 365 nm excitation source for 0.1 s; (d) Photographs of data encryption application by BA calcined product doped with different concentrations of CDs.

head.ce:title: Achieving tunable time-dependent delayed emission color via dynamic competition between the matrix and carbon dots for anti-counterfeiting and multidimensional encryption

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Hebei Key Laboratory of Photo-Electricity Information and Materials
College of Physics Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Delayed emission (DE) materials with tunable lifetime and wavelength can provide more modes of anti-counterfeiting and information encryption for improving security levels. It is both attractive and challenging to create carbon-based materials with dynamic DE colors through multiple luminescence centers. Herein, we have developed a composite material (BA@CDs) with tunable lifetimes and variable DE colors ranging from blue to orange by heating treatment method. Surprisingly, the delayed luminescence emission of BA@CDs can last up to 11 s, and then a color change occurs within 2 s. By utilizing various analysis methods, the origin of this long lifetime DE property is investigated, revealing the significant role played by two distinct phosphorescent emission centers. The dynamic competition and energy transfer (ET) process between the two emission centers reveal an interaction between the host matrix and N, S-CDs, indicating a significant interplay between them. This tunable phosphorescent material further demonstrates its advantages in anti-counterfeiting and information encryption.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Information security and confidentiality have become particularly important in recent years. With the continuous advancement of science and technology, information encryption and anti-counterfeiting technology have been the focus of attention in various fields. Fluorescent materials have been widely used in the fields of information encryption and anti-counterfeiting [1–3], but they have the disadvantages of requiring specific excitation wavelength, poor resistance to background noise, and being easily imitated. Compared to other materials, the delayed emission (DE) materials with long lifetimes have received much more attentions due to their unique property of sustained luminescence and their potential applications in information storage [4], bioimaging [5], data encryption [6,7], and multimode anti-counterfeiting. [8] The DE materials mainly include inorganic afterglow materials, organic phosphorescence (Phos) materials and thermally activated delayed fluorescence (TADF) materials [9–11]. However, most of them display only one emission color, if the color of the DE material can be dynamically adjusted, it will have a richer encryption mode for data protection, thereby further significantly improving the security level [12–17].

Carbon dots (CDs), a novel nanomaterial, have attracted significant interest for constructing metal-free DE materials owing to low toxicity, biocompatibility and remarkable optical features in past years [18–22]. The synthetic strategies and the choice of the precursor have a great influence on the DE performance of CDs. The different synthetic strategies of RTP CDs and their performance are summarized in Table S1 [23–29]. Some studies show that embedding CDs in various matrices, such as polyvinyl alcohol [26], silicone [30], aluminum hydroxide [31], urea [32], boric acid (BA) [33], BNO matrix [34], cyanuric acid [35], and some polymers, can effectively promote the ISC of CDs, inhibit non-radiative transition of triplet excitons and produce room temperature phosphorescence (RTP). However, various matrices will result in different DE phenomena. These provide us with a clue that the luminescence behavior of CDs can be regulated by different matrices. Although some attempts have been made recently, how to select suitable host matrix materials and conveniently prepare CDs materials with more colorful and easily adjustable DE remains a major challenge [36,37].

BA is an excellent phosphorescent matrix, and many studies have shown that it can effectively activate the DE of CDs [27,38,39]. In this work, we develop the water-soluble nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots (N, S-CDs) by hydrothermal method. And the composites (BA@CDs) are obtained by simple heating treatment the mixture of BA and N, S-CDs, which show excellent DE performance. The DE color of BA@CDs is dynamic, which varies with time, CDs content, excitation wavelength, or temperature. Fine analysis of the BA@CDs indicates the DE is mainly composed of RTP and TADF. The time-dependent evolutive DE color is mainly due to the DE from two luminescence centers with different lifetimes. Moreover, there is the first study of the dynamic competition between host and CDs. We provide a reasonable analysis and explanation of their dynamically tunable DE at different excitations, temperatures and concentrations of CDs. Rely on the superior DE property of BA@CDs, we achieve advanced optical anti-counterfeiting and triple-mode data encryption. Advanced dynamic DE can effectively avoid background interference, and provide a new dimension for anti-counterfeiting and encryption.

The synthesis of N, S-CDs using citric acid and thiourea as raw materials have been explored in some experiments [40,41]. We synthesize a water-soluble N, S-CDs for the first time that can be effectively excited in the visible light region (see the detail in Supporting Information). The Absorption (Abs), photoluminescence (PL), and photoluminescence excitation (PLE) spectra, shown in Fig. 1 (a), provide information about the optical characteristics of N, S-CDs. Monitoring for 523 nm, the PLE spectrum takes on a symmetry narrow band and the peak at 467 nm. The PL spectra of N, S-CDs display not only well symmetry but also excitation-dependence, and the optimal emission peak is at 523 nm under 467 nm excitation (Fig. 1(a), Fig. S1). Simultaneously, a bright green fluorescence (FL) emission can be observed under 455 nm light excitation (Fig. 1(a)). There are three peaks in the Abs spectra. The peaks at 204 nm and 266 nm are resulted from π-π* transition of C = C, C = O, respectively [41], and the other peak at 338 nm is attributed to the n-π* transition of the conjugated C = N or C = O bond [42]. It confirms the property of excitation-dependence is originated from the luminescence originating from various chemical bonds (Fig. S1) [43–45]. The FL average lifetime (τa ) can be well fitted by a double-exponential equation and the value of τa is 5.18 ns, as shown in Fig. S2 and Table S2.

In order to reveal the type of functional group and the surface chemical components of N, S-CDs, we measure Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrum and shown in Fig. 1 (b). The peaks at ∼ 3200 cm−1 and 2965 cm−1 are attributed to –OH and –CH2 stretching vibration, respectively. The sharp peak at 2046 cm−1 can be attributed to the -SCN bond [41]. The bending vibrations of C = C/N-H and –OH are responsible for the peaks at 1568 and 1376 cm−1, respectively. It is determined that the stretching vibration of C-O and C-N are responsible for the peaks at 1650 and 1170 cm−1, respectively. These hydrophilic groups make the N, S-CDs easily dissolve in water. The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images prove the N, S-CDs uniformly distribute with an average diameter of 3.2 nm (Fig. 1(c), Fig. S3). From the High-resolution TEM (HR-TEM) image, the clear fringe spacings of 0.23 nm can be observed, which is originated from the (100) planes of graphite carbon, indicating that the N, S-CDs have highly graphitic carbon structures (Fig. 1(c)). Furthermore, the X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern of the N, S-CDs exhibits a broad peak around 23° (Fig. S4), which matches well with the (002) planes of graphitic carbon, proving the N, S-CDs are successfully prepared. The Raman spectrum takes on two stronger signals at 1342 cm−1 and 1541 cm−1 attributing to the disordered structures or defects (D band) and the graphitic carbon domains (G band), and the intensity ratio (ID/IG) is about 0.9 indicating that the N, S-CDs have a high graphitization level (Fig. S5). The other peaks in Raman spectrum are derived from the amorphous heteroatom-cluster-rich surfaces [46].

The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra of the N, S-CDs confirm the existence of all the involved elements, i.e., C, N, S and O elements (Fig. S6). It can be seen from Fig. 1(d) that the high-resolution XPS spectrum of C1s consists two peaks at 284.8 and 285.9 eV, corresponding to C-C/C = C and C-S/C-N/C-O. The XPS spectrum of N 1 s can be separated into Pyrrolic N peak (399.2 eV) and Graphite N peak (400.3 eV), respectively (Fig. 1(e)). The XPS spectrum of S 2p also can be separated into two peaks at 163.7 eV and 164.9 eV, corresponding to S 2p3/2 and S 2p1/2, which is produced by spin–orbit splitting in the C-S covalent bond (Fig. 1(f)). The results suggest that the N, S-CDs are doping N and S elements, which is consistent with that of FT-IR spectrum and. The addition of heteroatoms (N, S) will enable the introduction of multi-energy level into N, S-CDs and further promote spin–orbit coupling for more efficient DE of N, S-CDs.

After heating treatment, the N, S-CDs is successfully embedded in the BA calcined product (the detailed experiment process is shown in Supporting Information), which is confirmed by the TEM image and HR-TEM patterns in Fig. 2 (a)-(b). From the HR-TEM image, N, S-CDs can be clearly observed in BA@CDs and the clear fringe spacings are measured to be 0.23 nm. The analysis of the element mapping for Fig. 2(c) indicates the element B is provided solely by BA and the element O is provided jointly by BA and N, S-CDs. And the element C, N, and S also uniformly distribute in the BA@CDs (Fig. 2(c)), explicitly demonstrating that the N, S-CDs are bound and well dispersed in the host matrix. In Fig. 2(d), the XRD pattern of the BA@CDs exhibits a broad peak at around 23°, in line with XRD pattern of N, S-CDs. And the characteristic peak at 27.7° can be attributed to B2O3. Additionally, the remaining peaks are believed to be formed impurities.

To further reveal the functional groups and chemical components of BA@CDs, we analyze its FT-IR spectrum and XPS spectra. In Fig. 2 (e), the FT-IR spectrum take on a broad absorption peak at 3200 cm−1, which is assigned to –OH vibrations. While, the characteristic peaks at 1408 cm−1, 1220 cm−1 and 880 cm−1 are originated from the stretching vibration of -BO, -BOH, -BC, respectively [33]. The B-C bond, a new covalent bond, can be formed between host matrix and N, S-CDs after heating treatment, further suggesting that N, S-CDs are combined with host matrix (Fig. 2(e)). The XPS spectra of as-prepared BA@CDs confirm the chemical state of all the involved elements, such as B, C, O, N and S elements (Fig. S7(a)). From the high resolution XPS spectrum of B1s in Fig. 2 (f), it can be separated into three bands peaked at 194.9, 193.8 and 192.7 eV, which corresponds to B-O, B2O3, and BCO2, respectively [33,47]. The C1s XPS spectrum consists of three peaks: 289.3, 286.3 and 284.7 eV, corresponding to C = O, C-O and C-C/C = C/C-B bands, respectively (Fig. 2(g)) [39]. Besides, the high-resolution XPS spectra of S 2p and N 1 s are also be measured, which further prove the existence of N, S-CDs in BA@CDs (Fig. S7(b)-(c)). Therefore, the aforementioned findings show that the main of host matrix is B2O3. The N, S-CDs and host matrix occur covalent coupling reactions that produce a B-C bond.

The DE spectra of N, S-CDs show almost weak DE (Fig. S10). Both of N, S-CDs solution and powder have low intersystem crossing efficiency due to the presence of water and oxygen, and their triplet states cannot be stabilized. In recent years, some studies have reported that the host matrix can provide an emerging glass state that can rigidify the triplet species of N, S-CDs, reduce nonradiative deactivation, and lower the energy gap (ΔE ST), effectively promoting ISC between S1 and T1 to produce Phos [27,47]. According to Fig. 3 (a), BA@CDs exhibits ultralong-lived DE after removing the 365 nm or 455 nm excitation light, which has a brief color change and last up to 11 s and 7 s, respectively (Video S1). The reason of DE color changing can be determined by studying the optical properties of BA@CDs. As shown in Fig. 3(b), the time-resolved DE spectra prove that the color of DE changes with time. At a delay time of 50 ms, the optimal peak position at 545 nm. With the delayed time controlled from 50 ms to 600 ms, the DE of BA@CDs significantly exhibits blue-shifted to 524 nm with the clear reduction of intensity. A part of the DE is caused by the N, S-CDs embedded in a B2O3-dominated matrix. In addition, we believe that there are other luminescent centers will also be effectively activated, which have different lifetimes, leading to the phenomenon of time-dependent DE [33].

The Fig. 3(c) shows the PL spectra of BA@CDs. Interestingly, it exhibits the multi-peak luminescent centers. Monitoring for 440 and 508 nm, the PLE spectra show different optimal excitations, further indicating the existence of multiple energy levels (Fig. S8). The longer-wavelength emission peak (low energy) and the shorter-wavelength emission peak (high energy) are designated as luminescent center-A (LC-A) and luminescent center-B (LC-B), corresponding to the trend of the two dashed lines in Fig. 3(c), respectively. The emission peak position of the LC-A is brought on minor red-shifted with increasing excitation wavelength. And following the excitation wavelength larger than 360 nm, the intensity of this peak become stronger and dominate in the PL spectra, which similar to the PL spectra of N, S-CDs (Fig. S1). It is proved LC-A is not newly formed but comes from N, S-CDs themselves. Compared to the LC-A, the PL of LC-B have greater degree of red-shifted with increasing excitation wavelength. As shown in Fig. 3(d), it exhibits an excitation-dependent property that DE wavelength changes from about 501 to 583 nm when excitation wavelength changes from 260 to 420 nm. And the absolute PL quantum yield of BA@CDs is 14.31 %. Furthermore, the DE spectra cannot fit a single Gaussian distribution, and multiple peaks will be directly observed in DE spectra under 460 nm excitation, which coincides with the PL spectra and implies that the existence of multiple singlet and triple states in this system. It is further validated that there are multiple luminescent centers in this system.

In some related reports, the pure BA material can generate DE phenomenon after heating treatment [48–50]. In order to further explore the luminescent mechanism of BA@CDs, pure BA (without adding N, S-CDs) is calcined under the same circumstances with BA@CDs. The BA calcined product is referred to as P-BA, which is also the host matrix of BA@CDs. The DE spectra of P-BA under various excitation wavelengths are depicted in Fig. S9, showing that the optimal excitation wavelength is 260 nm. The DE of P-BA also take on the multi peak emission, with short wavelength emission referred to as DE1. At the same time, we believe that the luminescence of the host matrix will also be reflected in luminescence of BA@CDs. As shown in the Fig. S11, we further compare the PL spectra of BA@CDs and P-BA under 260 nm, 360 nm and 420 nm excitation, respectively. These three excitation wavelengths represent three different periods. Under 260 nm-360 nm excitation, host matrix is effectively activated. And under 360 nm-420 nm excitation, the N, S-CDs is effectively activated. Firstly, at the excitation wavelength of 260 nm, the PL intensity of the P-BA is higher than that of BA@CDs, and the N, S-CDs almost unable to be effectively excited (Fig. S11(a)). The long wavelength FL peak in PL spectrum of P-BA is suit with the FL peak of LC-B, proving the LC-B origin from the host matrix (Fig. S11(a)). Subsequently, under 360 nm and 420 excitations, the PL intensity of the P-BA decreases while the intensity of BA@CDs exceeds the host matrix (Fig. S11(b)-(c)). It is suggested that the addition of N, S-CDs may promote the PL of the BA@CDs. In addition, it also reflected in the DE spectra. The DE1 and LC-B DE of P-BA is located at 431 and 481 nm and DE of BA@CDs is located at 505 nm under 260 nm excitation (Fig. 3(e)). It can prove that the ET process occurs between the host matrix and N, S-CDs by intensity of DE1 and LC-B DE decreasing and the Phos of LC-A increasing [51]. Under 360 and 420 nm excitation, the DE intensity of BA@CDs is significantly higher than that of P-BA (Fig. 3(e)-(f)). It is believed that N, S-CDs will promote the DE of the host matrix, and the main peak in the DE spectra is the superposition of the DE of the host matrix and N, S-CDs.

Meanwhile, the electron spin resonance (ESR) signals of BA@CDs and P-BA are observed at g = 2.0004, which can be identified as electrons trapped around oxygen vacancies (Fig. S12) [52]. The BA@CDs has a higher ESR intensity than P-BA, so we believe that the additional N, S-CDs provides more unpaired electrons. The weak conjugation between the unpaired electrons of O atoms in the boron-bonded confined space is identified as the origin of the DE of host matrix [38], which leads to the stronger DE intensity of the host matrix in BA@CDs than that of P-BA. The host matrix will promote the Phos emission of N, S-CDs, and in turn, and the N, S-CDs will also promote the DE of similar energy level in the host matrix. Therefore, we make a preliminary discovery that the DE spectra are composed by multiple peaks together and there is dynamic competition and ET between the LC-A generated by the N, S-CDs and the LC-B generated by the host matrix itself.

As the formation of triplet excimer is a typical kinetic process, it should be affected by temperature. To further investigate the origins and changes of each peak and verify the reliability of inference about the optical properties of BA@CDs, we measure the temperature-dependent steady-state DE spectra. As shown in Fig. 4 (a), under 420 nm excitation, the DE intensity of long-wavelength part decrease while that of short-wavelength part increase, in which the short-wavelength is attributed to the TADF and the long-wavelength is attributed to the Phos. The Fig. 4 (b) shows the change of integrating intensity and peak positions of DE spectra of BA@CDs. The location of peaks shift to high energy when the temperature excess 255 K due to the different emission has different change rule with the temperature. It can be seen from Fig. 4 (d) and (e), the intensity of the DE spectra decreases firstly and then increases with the temperature under 360 nm excitation, while the position of DE peak does not change from 105 K to 315 K. Compared to the DE spectra excited at 420 nm, the TADF emission is relatively weaker under 360 nm excitation, which consistent to the PL of the N, S-CDs and further confirms that TADF originates from the N, S-CDs. In Fig. 4(g) and 4(h), the intensity of DE decreases with the temperature increasing excited under 260 nm. It is believed that Phos dominates the entire spectrum under 260 nm excitation.

Based on the above analysis, it is believed that there are three main types of DE including the TADF and Phos emission of LC-A and the Phos of LC-B. As shown in Fig. 4(c), the room-temperature DE spectra can be fitted to three peaks at 497 nm, 560 nm and 587 nm, respectively. At the same time, the DE spectra excited under 360 nm and 260 nm are fitted to three peaks as Fig. 4 (c) and the positions of peaks are shown in Table S3. The DE of LC-B a major component in the whole spectra under excitation 260–360 nm, while the LC-A will gradually take up the majority when the excitation wavelength is larger than 360 nm. The effective excitation wavelengths consistent with the excitation wavelengths of the CDs and host. Due to the production of B-C bond, reducing the conjugation of N, S-CDs results the TADF of BA@CDs blue-shifted to about 495 nm. The Phos of LC-A at about 576 nm changes to 579 nm and 587 nm when the excitation wavelength varies from 260 nm to 420 nm. The Phos of LC-B at 484 nm changes to 515 nm and 560 nm with the various of excitation. The change rule of peak at about 495 nm and 576 nm consistent with that of N, S-CDs, while, that of 484 nm consistent with the matrix. Thus, the peaks at 497 nm, 560 nm and 587 nm is corresponding to the TADF and Phos emission of LC-A and the Phos of LC-B.

The DE spectra at temperatures of 105 K, 225 K and 315 K are fitted for better understanding the process of dynamic competition between LC-A and LC-B (Fig. S13, S14, S15 and Table S4). Firstly, under 420 nm excitation, the Phos of LC-A is 585 nm, while Phos of LC-B is near 560 nm at 105 K (Fig. S13(a)). As the temperature increasing to 315 K, the intensity of Phos emission gradually decreases, while the intensity of TADF emission around 497 nm gradually increases (Fig. S13(c)). Secondly, the Phos of LC-A and LC-B are 578 and 520 nm under 360 nm excitation at 105 K (Fig. S13(a)). With the increasing of temperature, TADF also appear and the emission peak position is slightly blue-shifted to 495 nm (Fig. S14). Additionally, the DE spectrum under the 260 4 nm excitation at 105 K is fitted to four emission peaks: the TADF and Phos from LC-A at 497 nm and 575 nm, respectively, as well as the DE of host matrix at 431 nm and the Phos of LC-B at 483 nm (Fig. S15(a)). And as the temperature increasing, the host matrix transfer energy and promote Phos and TADF of LC-A due to thermal vibration, ultimately leading to a significant reduction in the intensity of DE1 (Fig. S15). We conduct high-quality fitting curve for the DE spectra at different temperature and excitation wavelength, which provide reasonable positions for different emission peaks. The results of the all fitting DE spectra show that LC-A and LC-B exhibit distinct dynamic competition under different excitation lights and temperatures. Based on the above analysis, it further demonstrates that the long-wavelength LC-A origin from the N, S-CDs, while the short-wavelength LC-B origin from the host matrix and offer better supporting for the luminescent mechanism.

To better visualize the ET process and the competition between luminescence centers, the τa of Phos monitoring for the shorter wavelength (host matrix) and longer wavelength (N, S-CDs) are investigated and shown in Fig. 5 (a), (b), (c). The τa monitoring for 483 nm, 515 nm, 560 nm are 2.11, 1.50, and 0.96 s, excited under 260, 360 and 420 nm, respectively, while the τa monitoring for 575 nm, 579 nm, 587 nm decrease to 1.47, 0.94 and 0.86 s, respectively. From Table S5, it can be found that all the decay curve can be fitted to a triexponential function showing the feature of two ultralong lifetime Phos and one relatively short lifetime TADF. We further perform linear fitting on the τa of LC-A and LC-B at excitation wavelengths greater than 320 nm. The τa of LC-B decays faster (slope k1 = -0.015,k2 = -0.006), which show the Phos of LC-A gradually occupied the main position, and LC-B occupied the secondary position after wavelength red shift and further indicating the existence of dynamic competition between different luminescence centers (Fig. S16).

Based on the above analysis, the related origin about Phos and TADF of BA@CDs and whole process of electrons’ transfer are deduced and confirmed. We propose the activation and dynamic competition mechanism of RTP and TADF in BA@CDs. The Fig. 5 (d) shows the illustration of luminescent mechanism under high energy excitation (260–360 nm), in which the host matrix will occupy a more significant portion in DE. After BA@CDs is excited by appropriate energy, free electrons are formed in the conduction band (CB). Subsequently, a portion of free electrons relax to specific energy level and pass through the energy bands to generate FL. Meanwhile, other free electrons can be captured by oxygen vacancy defects in host matrix. After ceasing the excitation light, the trapped electrons can be released with thermal perturbation, then relax to specific energy level to excite weak DE1. In addition to this process mentioned above, electrons will continue to relax to the energy level of LC-B. Electrons will tend to transition back to the VB at the LC-B energy level rather than DE1. Besides, the host matrix also transfer energy to the N, S-CDs. Thus, a portion of the excited state (Sn) electrons go to the singlet excited state (S1) by IC and emit FL by radiative transition from S1 to ground state (S0). The electrons at S1 can be shifted to the lowest triplet excited state (T1) by ISC and IC, then to S0 for realizing effective Phos. And a part of electrons at triplet excited state also return to the S1 by reverse intersystem crossing (RISC), finally to the S0 for TADF.

The Fig. 5 (e) shows the illustration of luminescent mechanism under low energy excitation (360–460 nm), in which the N, S-CDs will occupy a more significant portion in DE. Similar to above discussion, the electrons of N, S-CDs are promoted from S0 to Sn, and relax to the S1 by IC and emit FL by radiative transition from S1 to S0. Electrons at S1 can also be transferred to T1 by ISC and then return to S0 appearing intense Phos. At the same time, it will also generate stronger TADF. In addition, free electrons in the excited state will also transfer to the matching e-trap in the host matrix, and then transfer to the energy level of LC-B, resulting LC-B DE. The Phos of host matrix and CDs will be dynamically competing with each other, which is the main reason for the DE color changing over time.

Owing to the excellent DE performance of BA@CDs, their potential applications in anti-counterfeiting and data encryption are demonstrated [41]. The BA@CDs act as a smart anti-counterfeiting material due to the outstanding time-dependent evolutive DE color. As shown in Fig. 6 (a), BA@CDs are filled in a specific model of Hebei University’s emblem pattern, demonstrating time-dependent DE properties (Video S2). Its color appears yellow immediately, then gradually turns to green within 2 s after 365 nm UV lamp switched off. The phenomena can be easily recognized by naked eyes.

Besides, the BA@CDs and a commercial fluorescent ink are configured a new type of anti-counterfeiting ink, together (see the detail in Supporting Information). As shown in Fig. 6(b), the letter “A” and “C” are written with fluorescent ink, while “B” and “D” are written using anti-counterfeiting ink on the paper. It is nearly impossible to discern accurate information from paper under daylight, but under 365 nm excitation light, all letters “A”, “B”, “C” and “D” appear. After turning off the light, only the “C” and “D” written with anti-counterfeiting ink can be effectively observed. Thus, it may be applied as a multi-level anti-counterfeiting mode, which creates more possibilities for future advancements in the field.

At the same time, we find the wavelength and τa of the DE can be adjusted adding different amounts of CDs (Fig. 6(c), Fig. S17, S18 and Table S6) [53]. Thus, multiple mode of data encryption is applied by achieving tunable DE color of BA@CDs. As shown in the Fig. 6(d), a multimode data encryption pattern “888″ is formed by BA@0 %CDs, BA@0.1 % CDs, BA@1 %CDs, and BA@5 %CDs (see the detail in Supporting Information, Video S3). In addition to time channel, excitation wavelength (254 nm, 365 nm and 455 nm) and temperature (150 K, 300 K and 380 K) channels are also added, which will achieve multidimensional data encryption. Taking the information code under 365 nm excitation and room temperature about 300 K as an example, it exhibits the information “888” after moving the 365 nm light, immediately. However, this coded information varies over time showing from “888” to “q6A” and then to “qbn” due to the variability of τa in different composites. By changing the excitation wavelength to 254 nm, the τa of BA@0 %CDs will significantly increase so that presenting different information from “888” to “A68” and eventually to “Ab0”. The complexes increase the afterglow time when the temperature is changed to 150 K under 365 nm excitation. Therefore, compared to the information changing from “888” to “q6A” at 300 K, the information is still “888” under this condition. Based on the three channels mentioned above, the designed pattern achieves six types of information (“888”,”98A”, “A68”, “Ab0”, “q6A” and “qbn”) and a total of 27 encoding methods, which effectively protects the information and opens up ideas for future applications of advanced data encryption. Such unique tunable DE properties of as-prepared material make it more difficult to be replicated or forged, which are supposed to be excellent and promising candidates for high-level anti-counterfeiting and data encryption.

In summary, a facile dual-component heating treatment strategy is proposed for synthesizing time-dependent DE color composite materials. The BA@CDs exhibits significant tunable DE colors changing from yellow to green within 2 s, and the final green emission persists for 11 s with the τa of 1.46 s under 365 nm excitation. Adding CDs to the B2O3-dominated matrix can not only change the optical characteristics of the CDs, significantly promote the TADF and Phos emission processes of the N, S-CDs, but also enhance Phos intensity and τa of the host matrix at the same time. The time-dependent Phos can be attributed to two main luminescent centers: the low-energy emission of N, S-CDs stabilized by the host matrix, and the high-energy emission of the host matrix promoted by N, S-CDs, resulting in distinguishable Phos colors and different τa . The ratio of the two Phos emission bands varies with time, triggering real-time evolution of the Phos color. By adjusting the excitation wavelength, temperature and amounts of N, S-CDs, multi-color DE can be effectively generated in the range of 431–600 nm and applied a three-channel data encryption. This study provides new insights into the synergistic effect between host matrix and N, S-CDs and luminescent mechanism of dynamic DE, providing a paradigm for in-depth understanding the dynamic DE process. The results will guide further development of host–guest materials based on CDs in the fields of Phos, TADF, anti-counterfeiting and data encryption and other areas.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was partly funded by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No.62175075, No.62075055, No. 61974009), the Central Project Guide local science and technology for development of Hebei Province (226Z1103G) and the Interdisciplinary research project of Hebei University (No. DXK202012). We also appreciate the technology support from the high-performance supercomputer center of Hebei University.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2023.147808.

ce:section.ce:para:

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z. Zhou, K. Jiang, N. Chen, Z. Xie, B. Lei, J. Zhuang, X. Zhang, Y. Liu, C. Hu, Room temperature long afterglow from boron oxide: A boric acid calcined product, Mater. Lett. 276 (2020) 128226. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.matlet.2020.128226.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Ultralong room-temperature phosphorescence from boric acid
